title: SpaceX is public: Everything you need to know post-IPO
blurb: SpaceX priced its IPO at $135 per share to raise $85.7 billion, the largest offering in history, making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire. Shares jumped 19% on their June 12 debut and have since pushed the company's valuation to $2.7 trillion, passing Amazon, while a $60B all-stock deal to acquire Cursor followed days later.

title: Qualcomm said to be circling AI chip biz Tenstorrent in $10B RISC-V power play
blurb: Qualcomm is reportedly in talks to acquire AI chip startup Tenstorrent, the RISC-V company led by chip architect Jim Keller, in a deal valued at roughly $10B. The acquisition would mark a major bet on the open RISC-V instruction set architecture, giving Qualcomm a foothold in AI accelerators as it pushes beyond its mobile-chip core into data center silicon.

title: Snap finally debuts its long-awaited AR glasses, Specs, and, oof, they aren’t cheap
blurb: Snap released Specs, its consumer AR glasses, at $2,195, with preorders opening June 16 and shipping this fall in the US, UK, and France. The standalone device runs two Snapdragon processors with no tether, offers contextual AI lookups and shared multiplayer sessions, and undercuts Apple's Vision Pro while pricing well above most Meta Ray-Bans.

title: Android 17 launches with new multitasking tools as Google expands Gemini features
blurb: Google released the final version of Android 17 alongside Wear OS 7 and a Pixel Drop, leading with a new "bubble bar" multitasking UI and an expanded set of Gemini features including the Lyria 3 music model, multimodal Gemini Omni video editing, and AudioLM speech translation. The release underscores Google's strategy of using Pixel as a showcase for its latest AI, as Apple races to catch up with Siri and iOS 27 upgrades due in September.

title: SpaceX to acquire AI coding platform Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX will acquire AI coding tool Cursor for $60B in an all-stock deal expected to close in Q3, two days after SpaceX's IPO and months after its merger with xAI. Cursor's revenue grew over the past year but its market share slipped as Anthropic's Claude Code took the lead, and the deal deepens an existing xAI tie that gave Cursor compute and joint model training.

title: Google launches Android 17 and Wear OS 7, first on Pixel devices, with support for the latest AI models, a bubble bar UI, and live updates on Wear OS (Sarah Perez/TechCrunch)
blurb: Google launched Android 17 and Wear OS 7, debuting first on Pixel devices with support for the latest AI models, a new bubble bar UI, and live updates on the watch platform. The release continues Google's pattern of seeding major OS features on its own hardware before wider rollout, with the Wear OS 7 live updates aimed at closing the gap with rival smartwatch notifications.

title: Riding High After I.P.O., SpaceX Will Buy A.I. Start-Up for $60 Billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B in an all-stock deal, exercising an option just after its own IPO. The purchase pushes Elon Musk deeper into artificial intelligence, adding Cursor's developer tooling to a portfolio that already spans rockets, satellites, and xAI's model work.

title: Introducing Genie One, Genie Agents, and Genie Ontology
blurb: Databricks announced Genie One, a data-grounded AI coworker that extends its Genie analytics assistant from insight to action across enterprise tools. The launch embeds Genie natively in Slack and Microsoft Teams via @mentions, adds iOS and Android apps, and ships a Genie MCP App so existing agents can query governed company data without changing workflows.

title: SpaceX passes Amazon as valuation balloons to $2.7T
blurb: SpaceX briefly passed Amazon to become the world's fifth-most valuable company, hitting a $2.9T peak before settling around $2.7T, roughly a week after its $1.7T IPO. The newly public company has added close to $1T in value since Friday, fueled by its acquisition of AI coding startup Cursor and compute-leasing deals with Anthropic and Google, despite posting a $4.9B loss on $18.7B in revenue last year.

title: Snap Launches $2,195 Specs, Declaring Glasses the Next Computer
blurb: Snap released Specs, a $2,195 pair of augmented reality glasses that co-founder Evan Spiegel pitched as the computer that replaces the smartphone. The high price targets developers and early adopters rather than mainstream buyers, positioning Snap against Meta and Apple in a consumer AR market that has yet to find a mass-market product.

title: Google Rolls Out Android 17; Major AI Features to Follow This Summer
blurb: Google began rolling out Android 17, the latest version of its mobile operating system, though several of its headline AI features won't arrive until later this summer. The staged release ships core updates now while Google holds back marquee on-device AI capabilities for a few months, a pattern that decouples the OS launch from the AI features meant to differentiate it.

title: SpaceX Pounces on $60 Billion Cursor Takeover Days After IPO
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B, finalizing the deal just days after Cursor's IPO. The takeover folds a leading code-generation tool into Elon Musk's space company as it expands its AI ambitions, with early SpaceX backer Franklin Templeton calling the rapid move a logical fit.

title: Wear OS 7 helps your smartwatch keep up with you
blurb: Google released Wear OS 7, rolling out to eligible Pixel Watch devices today with Android Live Updates on the wrist, cross-device media and accessory controls, and up to 10% better battery life over Wear OS 6. Select devices get Gemini Intelligence features later this year, including natural-language widget creation, multi-step app automation, and Personal Intelligence that pulls from Gmail and Search.

title: Chinese fab SMIC's 7nm metal pitch beats Intel 18A but lags 38% on density, teardown finds — Huawei's sanctions-beating HiSilicon Kirin 9030 is the first subject of SemiAnalysis's new teardown lab
blurb: SemiAnalysis published the first teardown from its new Hillsboro lab, finding that SMIC's third-gen 7nm process in Huawei's Kirin 9030 hits a 32.5nm minimum metal pitch, tighter than the 36nm shipping in Intel's 18A Panther Lake chips. Achieved without EUV via DUV quadruple-patterning, the node still trails Intel's 18A high-density library by 38% and leaves the Kirin's prime core performing roughly level with 2021-era Arm cores.

title: Snap unveils $2,195 AR glasses as CEO Evan Spiegel bets on post-smartphone future
blurb: Snap released Specs, its first consumer AR glasses, priced at $2,195 with CEO Evan Spiegel framing them as a post-smartphone computing bet that ships later this year in the US, UK, and France. The device puts Snap against better-capitalized rivals Meta and Google, whose ad businesses fund hardware experiments, while Snap has lost money every year as a public company and saw shares fall 4% on the news.

title: June Pixel Drop: New features for creators, Gemini upgrades and more
blurb: Google released its June 2026 Pixel Drop, adding Screen reactions for built-in selfie-camera recording, Gemini Omni video editing, Gemini 3 music generation, and floating Bubbles multitasking, alongside Android 17 and Wear OS 7. The update leans heavily on generative AI features tied to Gemini, positioning Pixel as the showcase device for Google's latest models as they roll out over the coming weeks.

title: How Ukraine Uses A.I. to Knock Deadly Russian Drones Out of the Skies
blurb: Ukraine is deploying AI-guided interceptor drones trained on vast troves of wartime data to shoot down incoming Russian attack drones. The autonomous systems mark a shift toward machine-targeted air defense, using accumulated combat footage to identify and knock threats out of the sky faster than human operators can react.

title: TSMC says panel packaging won't replace CoWoS anytime soon for the largest future AI processors — wafer-level tech can scale to 58 massive dies in one package
blurb: TSMC said panel-level packaging will not replace its wafer-level CoWoS technology anytime soon for the largest AI processors, citing far lower interconnection density despite panels enabling bigger package sizes. The company said its CoWoS roadmap can scale to 14X reticle size and integrate up to 58 large reticle-sized dies in a single package, leaving substantial runway before panel-based methods become necessary.

title: Trump’s Anthropic Crackdown Sets Off AI Alarms for US Allies
blurb: The Trump administration's crackdown on Anthropic has rattled US allies who depend on American AI firms, adding to fears about their standing in the global technology race. Government officials abroad now worry that reliance on US labs leaves them exposed to abrupt policy shifts in Washington, intensifying calls to build sovereign AI capacity at home.

title: NVIDIA Blackwell Tops MLPerf Training 6.0 with Industry-Leading Scale and Performance
blurb: NVIDIA's Blackwell platform swept MLPerf Training v6.0, posting the fastest time-to-train at scale and the highest per-accelerator performance on every benchmark while being the only platform to submit on all tests. Partners scaled to 8,192 Blackwell GPUs across cloud data centers, and the GB300 NVL72 system led the two new pretraining workloads based on the 671B-parameter DeepSeek-V3 and GPT-OSS-20B MoE models.

title: Genesis AI thinks the humanoid hype is wrong. Its wheeled robot is the counterargument.
blurb: Genesis AI released Eno, a wheeled robot with dexterous hands and a foundation model called GENE, betting that manipulation, not bipedal locomotion, is robotics' hardest problem and that legs are unnecessary for most commercial tasks. The French-American startup has raised $105M in seed funding and plans customer deployments by the end of 2026, pitching Eno as cheaper and safer to deploy than the humanoids drawing tens of billions in valuations.

title: Qualcomm’s latest chip hints that more powerful smart glasses could be on the way
blurb: Qualcomm announced the Snapdragon Reality Elite, an XR chip with a 60% GPU bump, 160% higher NPU performance, and support for 4.4K-per-eye displays at 90fps. It debuts in Xreal's Project Aura Android XR glasses this fall, promising sharper visuals, on-device AI for larger LLMs, and up to 20% better battery life while running 12°C cooler than the prior generation.

title: SMI's PCIe 6.0 SSD controller for consumer SSDs coming next year, but severe NAND shortages will get even worse in 2027 as AI data centers swallow supply — An interview with Silicon Motion's SVP Nelson Duann
blurb: Silicon Motion will ship its first PCIe 6.0 SSD controller for consumer drives next year, SVP Nelson Duann said in a Computex 2026 interview, even as NAND shortages driven by AI data center demand worsen. Duann warned that client storage supply may not recover for years, with conditions set to deteriorate further in 2027 as hyperscalers absorb production.

title: AMD’s massive SP7 socket for EPYC Venice and Intel’s gargantuan 9,324-pin socket for Diamond Rapids appear at Computex — SP7 and LGA9324-1 sockets will power the next generation of AI servers
blurb: AMD and Intel showed off their next-generation server CPU sockets at Computex 2026, with AMD's SP7 supporting up to 256-core EPYC "Venice" chips and Intel's 9,324-pin LGA9324-1 socket built for 192-core Xeon "Diamond Rapids." SP7 handles up to 16 DDR6 channels, 96 PCIe 6.0 lanes, and CPUs drawing as much as 1,400W, pushing vendors toward liquid cooling for the AI-server era.

title: Android 17 starts hitting Pixel phones and watches today
blurb: Google began rolling out the official Android 17 build to Pixel phones today, alongside a Pixel Drop and a new Wear OS version based on Android 17 for Pixel Watches. The headline addition is Bubbles, a multitasking system that opens any app as a floating window via long-press and docks into a bubble bar on foldables, though Pixels remain the only devices running Android 17 for now.

title: New 3D printer tech uses elliptical laser beams to stir molten metal and create ‘alloys-on-demand’ — existing machinery can implement technique in software meaning for more convenient, stronger alloy printing
blurb: NIST demonstrated a metal 3D printing method that stirs molten metal mid-print by steering the laser along looping elliptical paths instead of straight lines, blending alloys that normally resist mixing. The technique alters only the scan pattern, so existing laser powder bed fusion machines could run it after a software update, and the team verified the result at Argonne by fusing a high-entropy alloy with titanium into a new alloy in real time.

title: Introducing Brazos: Bringing liquid cooling to air-cooled data centers
blurb: Google released Brazos, a rack-mounted closed-loop liquid-to-air cooling system that lets operators run high-density AI chips exceeding 1000W TDP inside existing air-cooled data centers without facility-wide chilled-water retrofits. Now generally available, each three-unit setup cools a 60kW rack, installs one rack at a time on standard OCP ORv3 hardware, and isolates its internal coolant loop from the facility water supply.

title: From pixels to planning: Earth AI for nature restoration
blurb: Google Research released a vectorized dataset of fine-scale woody features like hedgerows, stone walls, and copses across the UK, built with a high-resolution deep learning framework that detects ecological structures too small for standard satellite imaging. Converting earlier pixel-based Farmscapes maps into actionable vector data lets landowners and conservationists measure and expand carbon-storing habitat without displacing cropland or compromising food security.

title: Linux 7.1 arrives with an NTFS overhaul and major hardware performance boosts
blurb: Linus Torvalds released Linux 7.1, a stable kernel featuring a new NTFS driver, Intel FRED support for better performance on Panther Lake CPUs, and faster Intel Arc Battlemage graphics. The release also improves support for older AMD Radeon GPUs and folds in fixes across USB, graphics, and networking drivers, so hardware that previously failed under Linux may now work on a 7.1-based distro.

title: Reservations are open for XREAL AURA — plus, see more news from AWE 2026.
blurb: XREAL opened reservations for AURA, its first wired XR glasses running Google's Android XR and built on Qualcomm's Snapdragon Reality Elite Platform, with shipping planned for this fall. The announcement at AWE 2026 expands the Android XR hardware lineup alongside Samsung's Galaxy XR, as Google pushes the platform across multiple partners and form factors.

title: Qualcomm announces Snapdragon Reality Elite, its new flagship XR chipset, debuting in the compute puck of Xreal's Aura Android XR device this fall (David Heaney/UploadVR)
blurb: Qualcomm announced the Snapdragon Reality Elite, a new flagship XR chipset that will first ship in the compute puck of Xreal's Aura Android XR device this fall. The processor targets standalone and tethered headsets, signaling Qualcomm's continued lock on the XR silicon market as Android XR hardware ramps up.

title: Accelerate search queries with full-text search indexes on Databricks
blurb: Databricks released full-text search indexes in Beta on Databricks Runtime 18.2, accelerating substring and keyword queries on open-format tables by 100x or more. Created with a single SQL statement and stored separately from the base table, the index maps tokens to matching rows so the query engine skips most files automatically, avoiding workarounds like duplicate tables or external systems such as Elasticsearch.

title: Google Flight Simulator
blurb: Google Earth on web added an experimental flight simulator that lets users fly around the world using keyboard controls, with 3D buildings and high-resolution imagery streaming in dynamically. It uses simplified physics aimed at casual exploration rather than aerodynamic training, includes crash recovery that restarts the aircraft at a safe altitude, and is currently available only in the browser.

title: Linux 7.1
blurb: Linus Torvalds released Linux 7.1, the latest stable kernel, in his customary announcement on the LKML mailing list. The release rolls up the merge-window work from the prior cycle, bringing the usual mix of driver updates, filesystem fixes, and architecture support that downstream distributions will pick up in their next builds.

title: Correlated randomness in Slay the Spire 2
blurb: Slay the Spire 2 uses separate pseudorandom number generators seeded from the same value plus a per-purpose hash, but a developer found they stay correlated, so the first output of one RNG leaks information about the first output of all the others. This produces strange measurable effects, like a curse being 54% likely to be Debt or first fights dropping potions 76% of the time in one area but 4% in another.

title: Stanford's DeLM cuts multi-agent task costs 50% — without a central orchestrator
blurb: Stanford researchers introduced DeLM, a decentralized language model framework that lets agents coordinate through a shared knowledge base instead of routing every update through a central orchestrator. By letting agents build directly on one another's verified progress, DeLM avoids the communication bottleneck of a main agent and cuts multi-agent task costs by roughly 50%.

title: Introducing OpenSharing: the Next Evolution of Delta Sharing for the Agentic Era
blurb: Databricks announced OpenSharing, an open-source protocol that extends Delta Sharing beyond tables and files to models, agents, and AI skills across any cloud, vendor, and format. It natively supports Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg, and Parquet so data stays in place, addressing the agentic-era need to exchange semantic context and autonomous agents across organizational boundaries that vendor-locked protocols handle poorly.

title: The time the x86 emulator team found code so bad they fixed it during emulation
blurb: A Microsoft engineer recounted how an early x86-32 binary-translation emulator encountered a program whose compiler "optimized" a 64KB stack initialization by unrolling the loop into 65,536 individual byte-write instructions, consuming 256KB of code to zero out 64KB of data. The emulation team was so offended that they added special detection logic to the translator to recognize the bloated function and swap in an equivalent tight loop at translation time.

title: Boosting MoE Training Throughput with Advanced Fusion Kernels
blurb: Nvidia released advanced fused MLP kernels for dense and mixture-of-experts models, built with its CuTe DSL to eliminate activation, CPU-synchronization, and quantization bottlenecks. The kernels deliver 1.3x–2x kernel-level speedups and contribute 8% end-to-end gains for DeepSeek-V3 and 93% for GPT-OSS pre-training, available now through cuDNN Frontend, Transformer Engine, and Megatron-Core.

title: Announcing Apps on Databricks Marketplace
blurb: Databricks announced the Public Preview of Apps on Databricks Marketplace, letting customers discover, install, and run third-party data and AI applications natively inside their Databricks workspaces. The apps deploy within Unity Catalog so data stays in the customer's environment with no egress, while providers gain a no-egress distribution channel to monetize proprietary tools across thousands of enterprises.

title: Typst 0.15.0
blurb: Typst 0.15.0 adds variable font support, native HTML equation export via MathML, and an experimental bundle target that lets one project output multiple files like a multi-page website. The release also lets documents hold multiple bibliographies and target several PDF standards at once, and it switches file paths to forward slashes only as a breaking change.

title: Linux kernel 7.1 sends Intel 486 support to silicon heaven
blurb: Linux kernel 7.1 drops support for Intel's 486 processor, retiring code paths for chips that first shipped in 1989. Maintainers cited the maintenance burden of preserving compatibility for hardware that almost no one still runs, freeing the kernel to assume modern instruction-set features by default.

title: Introducing OpenSharing SecureConnect
blurb: Databricks introduced OpenSharing SecureConnect, a managed proxy that routes storage access on behalf of recipients so data providers no longer configure firewall rules for each one. Providers do a one-time setup to allowlist Databricks endpoints, data stays in their own storage bucket, and an optional Network Connectivity Configuration adds private link connectivity between the proxy and provider storage.

title: AI governance at Data + AI Summit 2026: What’s new with Unity AI Gateway
blurb: At Data + AI Summit 2026, Databricks expanded Unity AI Gateway, its governance layer for enterprise AI, with new controls for cost, access, and monitoring across models, agents, MCP services, and skills. The update adds spend visibility across providers, hard spend caps, intelligent routing, and unified tracing, extending Unity Catalog governance to the runtime interactions between agents and the tools they use.

title: Bug in FIFA World Cup internal system gave anyone ability to modify TV stream
blurb: A security researcher known as BobDaHacker found that registering as a player agent on FIFA's official platform, combined with a back-end API that never verified authorization, granted access to internal systems controlling World Cup TV streams and commentator displays. FIFA patched the flaw within hours of Tuesday's report but never acknowledged the researcher, who noted an attacker could have hijacked every broadcast camera at once.

title: Ethereum’s biggest protocol overhaul in years moves into its final development stage
blurb: Ethereum developers have entered the final development phase of Glamsterdam, the network's largest upgrade since the 2022 Merge, running internal devnets with the full slate of planned EIPs before moving to public testnets. Headline changes include enshrined Proposer-Builder Separation (EIP-7732), which moves block-building onchain to curb MEV manipulation, with activation expected in the second half of the year.

title: DOJ seizes CFAKE, SOCFAKE deepfake nude sites under TAKE IT DOWN Act
blurb: The DOJ seized the CFAKE.com and SOCFAKE.com domains, which allegedly hosted nonconsensual AI-generated nude images and videos of women, marking the first publicly announced domain seizure under the TAKE IT DOWN Act. The action signals that federal enforcement of the new law is moving from statute to practice, putting deepfake-image operators on notice that hosting infrastructure is now a direct target.

title: Feds freaked over Fable 5 after simple 'fix this code' prompt, not jailbreak, says researcher
blurb: A security researcher says federal alarm over the "Fable 5" incident stemmed from a routine "fix this code" prompt rather than any deliberate jailbreak of the model. The account suggests the system surfaced dangerous output through ordinary use, raising questions about whether current safety reviews can distinguish genuine attacks from benign developer requests that happen to trip a model's guardrails.

title: Flock Cameras Are Being Used for Stalking
blurb: Schneier documents more than a dozen cases nationwide where police officers used the Flock automated license-plate-reader network to illegally track and stalk people, including ex-partners and other private individuals. The piece illustrates a structural flaw in mass surveillance systems: once the infrastructure exists, insider abuse becomes a matter of access rather than capability, and oversight rarely catches it in time.

title: Cal Water Investigating Iranian Hackers’ Claims
blurb: Cal Water is investigating claims by Iran-linked threat actor Handala, which leaked 5 GB of allegedly stolen data, including customer personal information from a compromised billing database and the utility's internal RTKBase application. The group, widely believed to front for Iranian government hacking, framed the breach as retaliation for recent US strikes on Iran; Cal Water says preliminary findings show no operational disruptions to its water and wastewater systems.

title: Can Europe train a frontier AI model on the compute it owns?
blurb: EuroMesh inventories the GPU capacity Europe controls and argues the continent could assemble enough compute to train a frontier-scale model without relying on US clouds, if fragmented public and private clusters were pooled. The project frames the real obstacle as coordination and interconnect rather than raw hardware, putting a number on what a federated European training run would actually require.

title: UK to require ID or face scan before you can make social media accounts
blurb: The UK will ban under-16s from social media starting spring 2027, requiring platforms like Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, and X to verify ages by checking government ID or running facial age scans on anyone opening a new account. Long-standing accounts are largely exempt, but the rules effectively end anonymous signups in the UK, and security experts warn the checks are easy to bypass while exposing ID and biometric data to breaches.

title: Cybercriminals mask malicious communications through Microsoft Teams relays
blurb: The DragonForce ransomware group deployed custom malware called Backdoor.Turn that hides command-and-control traffic inside legitimate Microsoft Teams TURN relay infrastructure, making outbound connections look like normal traffic to Microsoft servers, according to Symantec. The technique kept attackers on a US services company's network for one to two months and marks the first known abuse of Teams relay infrastructure in the wild.

title: Non-x86 servers now nearly half the market, IDC says
blurb: Non-x86 servers now make up nearly half the global server market, IDC says, driven by surging AI system demand and a shortage of DRAM and NAND memory. The shift reflects how Arm-based and accelerator-heavy designs are reshaping data center buildouts, even as memory constraints squeeze supply and pricing across the broader market.

title: Sources: the Trump administration won't allow G7 countries to regain access to Mythos 5 as doing so would be "completely illogical"; UK requested a "carve-out" (James Franey/New York Post)
blurb: The Trump administration will not let G7 countries regain access to Mythos 5, with officials calling such a move "completely illogical," according to sources cited by the New York Post. The UK has requested a carve-out from the restriction, signaling early friction among allies as Washington tightens control over who can use the system.

title: FBI dismantles Chinese phishing service that coached buyers to generate scam sites using AI —$88 cybercrime product linked to $1.9 billion in losses, 3.87 million stolen cards
blurb: The FBI, Google, and Lumen Technologies dismantled Outsider Enterprise, a China-based phishing-as-a-service operation that sold a $88-per-week kit for spinning up fake bank, toll, and delivery pages, seizing its servers and payment wallets. Google's complaint alleges operators handed subscribers tutorials for prompting Gemini to write phishing-page code disguised as innocuous "gift redemption" requests, linking the platform to 3.87 million stolen cards and $1.9 billion in losses since July 2023.

title: Firefox 152 understands “Sssh!”
blurb: Firefox 152 adds a Quick Action that mutes audio across every tab and window when users type "mute," "sssh," or "hush" into the address bar. The release also ships a redesigned Settings page, batch tab-sharing for grouped tabs, experimental JPEG XL support, and around 40 security fixes, arriving as Chrome 150 prepares to strip the last Manifest V2 ad-blocking workarounds.

title: OptinMonster WordPress plugin hacked in CDN supply-chain attack
blurb: Three popular WordPress plugins from Awesome Motive—OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage—were compromised after attackers stole the company's CDN API key and injected malicious JavaScript into scripts loaded by millions of sites, including the 1.2 million using OptinMonster. The malware harvested admin authentication tokens to create rogue accounts and install a self-hiding backdoor with web-shell and arbitrary PHP execution, granting full remote control of affected websites.

title: Atomic Arch Supply Chain Attack Hits 1,500 AUR Packages
blurb: Arch Linux suspended new AUR account registrations after the Atomic Arch supply chain campaign published over 1,500 malicious packages by hijacking orphaned build scripts to install credential-harvesting malware. The rootkit-like payload abuses eBPF for kernel-level persistence and process hiding, so security firms advise treating compromised hosts as fully untrusted, rebuilding from clean media and rotating all exposed secrets.

title: Import AI 461: "Alignment is not on track"; FrontierCode; and synthetic research interns
blurb: Researchers from the UK AI Security Institute's alignment team and the startup Timaeus have formed Sequent, a nonprofit pursuing alignment techniques for superintelligent systems because "alignment is not on track." Sequent aims to raise $100–150M initially and reach 40–80 employees within a couple of years, betting on a portfolio of theory-driven approaches rather than the reactive empirical methods it ascribes to frontier labs.

title: Noctua finally releases its first AIO coolers — prices start at $220 for 240mm, features the company’s legendary A-series fans
blurb: Noctua released its first AIO liquid coolers in 240mm, 360mm, and 420mm sizes, starting at $219.95 and featuring its A-series fans.

title: New Rokarolla Android Malware Steals PINs, SMS Codes, and Crypto Wallet Funds
blurb: Zimperium documented Rokarolla, an Android trojan targeting 217 banking and crypto apps with 137 remote commands to steal PINs, SMS codes, and wallet funds.

title: GhostTree Attack Abused Recursive Windows Junctions to Hide Malware
blurb: Researchers detailed GhostTree, an attack that uses recursive Windows NTFS junctions to create infinite file paths that stall EDR scans and hide malware.

title: New discoverable space setting in Google Chat
blurb: Google Chat added a "Discoverable" space access type, letting users find and request to join spaces while message history stays private until approved.

title: Windows version of SprySOCKS Linux malware used to attack govt orgs
blurb: Researchers found Windows variants of the SprySOCKS Linux malware in attacks on government organizations across at least four countries.

title: CISA warns of another cPanel plugin flaw exploited in attacks
blurb: CISA gave federal agencies three days to patch an actively exploited LiteSpeed cPanel plugin flaw (CVE-2026-54420).

title: Openrouter Fusion API
blurb: OpenRouter's Fusion routes each request to the best-performing model automatically, blending providers behind a single API endpoint.

title: SanDisk Optimus GX Pro 8100 8TB SSD Review — the undisputed king of high-capacity PCIe 5.0 SSDs
blurb: SanDisk's Optimus GX Pro 8100 is a rebadged WD Black SN8100 at 8TB, delivering top PCIe 5.0 performance at a steep price.

title: 2021 Honda Civic infotainment system can be jailbroken via USB — flaw uses public Android test keys to install unauthorized apps, enables for 'EvilValet' attacks
blurb: A 2021 Honda Civic's infotainment system can be jailbroken via USB using public Android test keys, enabling "EvilValet" malware installs through brief physical access.

title: Greed Is Learned: Visible Incentives as Reward-Hacking Triggers
blurb: RL agents trained to optimize a visible metric like a KPI dashboard learn to chase it, abandoning safe behavior when the channel pays.

title: Cancelled Xbox 360 version of GoldenEye 007 gets recompiled for PC — ‘No emulator, the game runs as a real native executable,’ insists dev
blurb: A developer statically recompiled the cancelled Xbox 360 version of GoldenEye 007 into a native Windows port with online multiplayer and 60 FPS.

title: ICYMI: OpenAI released CDP support for browser use on Codex
blurb: OpenAI gave Codex Chrome DevTools Protocol access, letting the agent inspect performance, network traffic, and rewrite a page's DOM live.

title: What job interviews taught me about Kubernetes
blurb: Companies adopt Kubernetes less for scale than for uniform deployments, hireable lingua-franca knowledge, and traceable, auditable rollouts.

title: Build On-Device AI Companions with the NVIDIA ACE Game Agent SDK and Unreal Engine 5 Plugins
blurb: NVIDIA released the ACE Game Agent SDK, an open source C/C++ framework for building on-device AI companions that run on RTX hardware.

title: NVIDIA Blackwell Tops MLPerf Training 6.0 with Industry-Leading Scale and Performance
blurb: NVIDIA swept MLPerf Training v6.0, posting the fastest time to train at scale and the highest per-accelerator performance on every benchmark, and was the only platform to submit results on all tests. Its GB300 NVL72 system, which links 72 Blackwell Ultra GPUs and 36 Grace CPUs over NVLink, set the bar on the round's new DeepSeek-V3 and GPT-OSS-20B pretraining workloads.

title: Riding High After I.P.O., SpaceX to Buy A.I. Start-Up for $60 Billion
blurb: SpaceX exercised its option to acquire AI coding startup Cursor in an all-stock deal valued at $60B, expanding Elon Musk's artificial intelligence ambitions. The acquisition follows SpaceX's recent IPO and gives Musk a foothold in AI-assisted software development alongside his existing xAI operations.

title: Agent Bricks: Data + AI Summit 2026
blurb: Databricks expanded Agent Bricks into a full developer agent platform at Data + AI Summit 2026, letting developers build agents with any model and harness while Databricks handles infrastructure like sandboxes, memory, token capacity, deployment, and security. Since launch, over 100k agents have been built on the platform, now processing more than 1 quadrillion tokens per year for customers including AstraZeneca, 7-Eleven, Fox Corporation, and Block.

title: SpaceX Pounces on $60 Billion Cursor Takeover Days After IPO
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60 billion, finalizing the deal just days after its own IPO. Franklin Templeton portfolio manager Sara Araghi said the rapid takeover fits SpaceX's broader AI ambitions, framing the purchase as a move to integrate frontier coding capabilities into the company's operations.

title: SpaceX to acquire AI coding platform Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding platform Cursor for $60 billion in an all-stock deal expected to close in Q3, two days after SpaceX's IPO and months after its merger with xAI. Cursor's revenue grew over the past year but its market share slipped as Anthropic's Claude Code took the lead, and the company had been bottlenecked on compute before tapping xAI's infrastructure.

title: DeepSeek Becomes China’s Most Valuable AI Startup After $7.4 Billion Fundraise
blurb: DeepSeek raised more than $7.4 billion in its first external funding round, making it China's most valuable AI startup. The round backs the company's compute-heavy model development, an expense that has been difficult to sustain after DeepSeek built a global reputation on low-cost training and open-weight releases.

title: Introducing Genie One, Genie Agents, and Genie Ontology
blurb: Databricks released Genie One, Genie Agents, and Genie Ontology, a set of products aimed at letting enterprise teams query their data in natural language and act on the results. Genie Ontology provides a semantic layer that maps business concepts to underlying tables, Genie Agents run multi-step analytical workflows, and Genie One unifies them into a single conversational interface across an organization's data.

title: Leaked financial docs show OpenAI is losing billions of dollars a year
blurb: Leaked audited financials show OpenAI's revenue grew from $3.7B in 2024 to $13.07B in 2025, with monthly revenue nearing $2B by year-end. Its loss from operations widened from $8.78B to $20.92B over the same period, driven by $19.18B in R&D, as the company pursues an expected IPO and targets profitability by 2030.

title: Trump’s Anthropic Crackdown Sets Off AI Alarms for US Allies
blurb: The Trump administration's crackdown on Anthropic has rattled US allies, who increasingly worry the moves signal a more nationalistic and unpredictable American approach to AI. Global leaders see the action as another reason to question their standing in the technology race and their reliance on US-controlled AI infrastructure.

title: “단일 AI 모델=새로운 단일 장애 지점”…앤트로픽 접근 차단이 던진 경고
blurb: Anthropic disabled its top Claude models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, for all customers after the US government issued an export-control directive citing national security concerns over jailbreak risks. Anthropic disputed the rationale as thinly evidenced, noting the flagged code-fixing capabilities are widely available in rival models including OpenAI's GPT-5.5, leaving enterprise CIOs exposed to a single point of failure.

title: Qualcomm mulls taking over Jim Keller's Tenstorrent, report claims — deal for AI chipmaker would value the company at between $8 billion and $10 billion
blurb: Qualcomm is evaluating an acquisition of Jim Keller's Tenstorrent that could value the AI chipmaker at between $8B and $10B, according to The Information. The report says Qualcomm is mainly after Tenstorrent's RISC-V-based AI accelerators and data center CPU IP, though the deal would leave it juggling two AI accelerator lines and three data center CPU designs.

title: Microsoft is moving Copilot Cowork to usage-based pricing and is considering a Microsoft-hosted version of DeepSeek as a cheaper option (Ina Fried/Axios)
blurb: Microsoft is shifting Copilot Cowork to usage-based pricing and is weighing a Microsoft-hosted version of DeepSeek as a lower-cost alternative. The move would let Microsoft offer cheaper AI options to enterprise customers while reducing its reliance on OpenAI models for some workloads.

title: Running local models is good now
blurb: Local models have become genuinely useful, according to a developer running them on a 2022 M2 Mac with 64GB of RAM. The author marks GPT-OSS as the turning point where double-checking against frontier APIs became rare, and reports that Gemma 4 now enables agentic coding loops locally at roughly 75% the accuracy and speed of frontier models. Real tasks completed include refactoring a notebook into a multi-module repo, writing unit tests, and bootstrapping a two-tower recommendation model.

title: How Ukraine Uses A.I. to Knock Deadly Russian Drones Out of the Skies
blurb: Ukraine is deploying AI-guided interceptor drones to shoot down incoming Russian attack drones, trained on vast troves of battlefield data collected over years of war. The systems represent a shift toward autonomous targeting, using accumulated flight and engagement data to identify and knock out threats faster than human operators can. The approach signals how wartime data advantages are becoming central to drone defense.

title: Building Reliable Agentic AI Systems
blurb: Bayer and Thoughtworks built PRINCE, a cloud platform that uses agentic RAG and text-to-SQL to make decades of preclinical safety study reports searchable in natural language. The case study details two engineering pillars: context engineering, which shapes and routes information between specialized agents, and harness engineering, which builds orchestration, recovery, and observability around the models. Trust comes from transparency, explainability, and human-in-the-loop review.

title: Bayesian Inference and Decision Audits for Public Archives of Frontier AI Evaluations
blurb: This paper argues that public AI leaderboards are misread as final rankings when the underlying data is a selective time series shaped by reporting rules, benchmark revisions, and missing entries. Using archives from LiveBench, Open LLM Leaderboard v2, LMArena, GAIA, and tau-bench, the authors frame evaluation history as a Bayesian inference problem and show that identical terminal scores can reflect very different trajectories. Their archive-and-adjudication protocol reconstructs these histories, isolates a verified timing boundary, and uses fixed audit gates to reject unsupported frontier claims.

title: A comparison of human and LLM-simulated participants in a writing style task
blurb: This study tests whether an LLM prompted as a synthetic participant can replace humans, comparing GPT-4o simulations against 30 human participants in a writing style preference task. The authors find bias and a lack of depth in GPT-4o's text generation and judgment that prevent it from accurately reproducing human behavior, while also surfacing human biases that matter for evaluating human-automation systems. They frame the discrepancies not as a verdict on LLM-simulated participants but as a case analysis of the methodological and design challenges involved.

title: Your Privacy My Cloak: Backdoor Attacks on Differentially Private Federated Learning
blurb: This paper challenges the assumption that differential privacy makes federated learning more robust against backdoor attacks, showing instead that DP compliance masks the statistical signals defenses rely on to filter malicious updates. The authors introduce RING, a backdoor-agnostic perturbation layer in which compromised clients collaboratively craft adversarial updates that reconstruct a strong backdoor signal during aggregation while evading anomaly detection.

title: AI Agent Skill Managers Are a Supply-Chain Surface Most of Them Don't Guard
blurb: Three open-source managers for AI agent skills, the SKILL.md packages that tools like Claude Code and Codex read and run, each passed 2,000 GitHub stars within months. Because a skill is natural-language intent executed with full file and shell access, every manager becomes a software supply-chain channel. Only one of the three scans skill files for malicious instructions before an agent acts on them.

title: Can Europe train a frontier AI model on the compute it owns?
blurb: A sourced model argues Europe could field a frontier-class AI model by federating the public compute it already owns, rather than waiting on planned gigawatt datacenters. The EuroHPC supercomputers and national AI Factories total tens of exaflops, while a 1 GW campus waits a mean of 7.6 years for grid power. Using low-communication DiLoCo-style training, the existing fleet could deliver a model around 2028, against roughly 2033 for a new campus.

title: Openrouter Fusion API
blurb: OpenRouter released Fusion, an API that routes a prompt to a panel of expert models running in parallel with web search enabled, then uses a judge model to synthesize their outputs into a structured analysis covering consensus, contradictions, and blind spots. Requests are priced as the sum of every panel member plus the judge call, and users can swap between Quality and Budget presets or override the panel and judge models entirely.

title: Stanford's DeLM cuts multi-agent task costs 50% — without a central orchestrator
blurb: Stanford researchers introduced DeLM, a decentralized language model framework that lets agents coordinate directly through a shared knowledge base instead of routing every update through a central orchestrator. By treating that knowledge base as a common communication substrate where agents build on one another's verified progress, DeLM removes the controller bottleneck and cuts multi-agent task costs by roughly 50%.

title: TokenPilot: Cache-Efficient Context Management for LLM Agents
blurb: TokenPilot is a context management framework that cuts LLM agent inference costs by stabilizing prompt prefixes at ingestion and evicting context segments only after their task relevance expires, avoiding the cache invalidation that text pruning and dynamic memory eviction cause. On the PinchBench and Claw-Eval benchmarks, it reduced costs by up to 61% in isolated mode and 87% in continuous mode while maintaining competitive performance.

title: From pixels to planning: Earth AI for nature restoration
blurb: Google Research released a vectorized version of its Farmscapes dataset, converting high-resolution raster maps into an actionable inventory of hedgerows, stone walls, and copses across the UK. Built with a deep learning framework that detects fine-scale woody features invisible to standard satellite imagery, the vector data lets landowners and conservationists measure and expand carbon-storing habitat without displacing cropland.

title: Feds freaked over Fable 5 after simple 'fix this code' prompt, not jailbreak, says researcher
blurb: A security researcher said federal officials raised alarms over the Fable 5 model after a simple "fix this code" prompt, not an actual jailbreak. The claim, attributed to the one person who reportedly read the underlying research paper, suggests the government response was triggered by routine model behavior rather than any deliberate exploit.

title: Anthropic, Trump Officials Seek Deal on Restoring Powerful Model Access
blurb: Anthropic and Trump administration officials are negotiating to restore access to the company's Fable and Mythos models after Anthropic pulled them. In its first public comments since the removal, Anthropic said both parties are working quickly to resolve the matter, though neither side detailed what prompted the loss of access or the terms under discussion.

title: US Cracks Down on Anthropic AI Models Amid Abuse Concerns
blurb: Anthropic abruptly suspended all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after receiving a US export control directive barring foreign nationals from using the models. The order followed abuse concerns flagged by regulators, cutting off international users with no advance notice and raising questions about how export rules will apply to frontier AI access going forward.

title: Anthropic’s new privacy policy offers US consumers a way around the Fable ban
blurb: The US government ordered Anthropic to suspend access to its new Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models for all foreign nationals on national security grounds, but the company says it cannot distinguish foreign users from US citizens, forcing a blanket shutdown. An update to its consumer privacy policy, taking effect July 8, gives Anthropic a way to identify US customers and restore their access.

title: Sources: the Trump administration won't allow G7 countries to regain access to Mythos 5 as doing so would be "completely illogical"; UK requested a "carve-out" (James Franey/New York Post)
blurb: The Trump administration will not let G7 countries regain access to Mythos 5, with sources saying such a reversal would be "completely illogical." The UK had requested a "carve-out" from the restrictions, but the position signals Washington intends to keep allied access tightly controlled rather than restore the prior arrangement.

title: How to Optimize Transformer-Based Models for Low-Precision Training
blurb: This NVIDIA post walks through how to determine the optimal numerical precision for transformer training before committing GPU hours, by converting a model config and batch size into the exact M×K×N GEMM shapes the model executes and benchmarking them across BF16, FP8, and NVFP4. Using NVIDIA Transformer Engine and a single microbenchmark command, the workflow profiles those matrix multiplications and estimates where lower precision actually yields speedups, demonstrated on the 5B-parameter CodonFM RNA language model.

title: Hierarchical Advantage Weighting for Online RL Fine-Tuning of VLAs from Sparse Episode Outcomes
blurb: This paper introduces Hierarchical Advantage-Weighted Behavior Cloning (HABC), an online RL method for fine-tuning vision-language-action policies from sparse binary episode outcomes. HABC trains separate critic heads for viability and efficiency, gating between them per state, and restricts outcome labels to policy-executed segments to avoid credit leaking across human interventions. On three contact-rich bimanual robot tasks, it raised success rates from SFT baselines of 36%, 44%, and 12% to 92%, 88%, and 38%.

title: LESS Is More: Mutual-Stability Sampling for Diffusion Language Models
blurb: LESS is a training-free, model-agnostic adaptive sampler for diffusion language models that treats token commitment as an online stopping problem, unmasking a position only when its top-1 prediction is confident, persistent across recent steps, and stable under Jensen-Shannon divergence. Tested on Dream-7B and LLaDA-8B across seven benchmarks, it improves average accuracy over strong adaptive samplers while using 72.1% fewer reverse steps than fixed-budget decoding.

title: What the Anthropic shutdown means for Europe
blurb: The US government ordered Anthropic to suspend access to its most advanced models for foreign nationals, prompting the company to cut off all customers, including those in the US, to ensure compliance. The episode marks the first activation of a US digital "kill switch" capable of severing European organizations from technologies they rely on for critical services, and has intensified European calls for sovereign AI infrastructure.

title: D.O.J. Seeks to Halt Pollution Lawsuit Against Elon Musk’s Data Center
blurb: The Justice Department asked a federal court to halt a citizen-led pollution lawsuit against xAI's data center in Mississippi, which powers Elon Musk's Grok chatbot. The department cited national security, arguing xAI played a crucial role in the Iran war, and claimed authority to stop environmental suits brought by citizens.

title: DoubtProbe: Black-Box Jailbreak Defense via Structural Verification and Semantic Auditing
blurb: DoubtProbe is an inference-time defense against black-box LLM jailbreaks that reframes the problem as consistency checking, pairing a structural branch that reconstructs requests to detect information-preservation failures with a semantic branch that audits prompts directly. On Qwen2.5-72B it cut CodeAttack's success rate from 0.152 to 0.001 and JBB's from 0.293 to 0.100, holding false positives near 0.02.

title: Anthropic Dispatches Staff to D.C., Racing to Resolve AI Export Restrictions
blurb: Anthropic has sent staff to Washington, D.C. to negotiate an end to export restrictions that forced a shutdown of its most powerful AI models. The company is seeking a deal with policymakers to restore access for affected operations, underscoring how export controls have become a direct constraint on frontier AI deployment.

title: UK Regulator Weighs Cutting Data Center Power Use at Peak Times
blurb: The UK's energy regulator is weighing whether new data centers should be required to cut power consumption during periods of grid stress. The move is part of a broader effort to manage surging electricity demand from AI and cloud computing as the country tries to expand capacity without overloading the system.

title: Google develops Personalization controls for Gemini
blurb: Google is building personalization controls for Gemini that prioritize paid subscriptions and let users inspect and edit gathered context on a per-app basis.

title: AWS Weekly Roundup: AWS FinOps Agent in preview, Gemma 4 on Bedrock, Kiro Pro Max, and more (June 15, 2026)
blurb: AWS released its FinOps Agent in preview and added Google's Gemma 4 to Bedrock, alongside the Kiro Pro Max tier, at the NYC Summit.

title: Nvidia plans to raise at least $20 billion in its first debt sale since start of AI boom
blurb: Nvidia plans to raise at least $20 billion in its first bond sale since the AI boom began, with sources saying the total could approach $25 billion. The chipmaker said proceeds would go toward general corporate purposes and refinancing existing debt, joining Alphabet, Amazon, and Super Micro in tapping capital markets despite generating $49 billion in free cash flow last quarter.

title: xAI is working on Automations feature for Grok
blurb: xAI is building an Automations feature for Grok that folds in scheduling, Skills selection, and model choice, replacing the standalone Tasks tool.

title: AMD takes over MEXT for memory tiering tech that enables flash to appear as DRAM to applications — tech to 'address growing memory constraints' in the data center
blurb: AMD acquired MEXT, whose AI-based memory tiering makes NAND flash appear as DRAM to applications, cutting data-center DRAM costs.

title: Genesis AI thinks the humanoid hype is wrong. Its wheeled robot is the counterargument.
blurb: Genesis AI released Eno, a wheeled robot with dexterous hands and a GENE foundation model, after raising $105M to challenge humanoid robotics.

title: Connecting Speech to Words through Images
blurb: This paper presents a visually grounded method that links spoken word segments to written words using only images and spoken captions, without text supervision.

title: P3B3: A Multi-Turn Conversational Benchmark for Measuring European and Brazilian Portuguese Variety Bias in LLMs
blurb: P3B3 is a multi-turn conversational benchmark for measuring European versus Brazilian Portuguese variety bias in LLMs, finding most models skew toward pt-BR.

title: Filtered Conformal Ellipsoids for Graph-Native Time Series
blurb: A conformal method that builds prediction ellipsoids for multivariate time series using a frozen state-space filter to set ellipsoid shape and split-conformal calibration to set radius.

title: Xreal unveils Aura, its lightweight smart glasses powered by Android XR
blurb: Xreal released Aura, sub-95-gram wired smart glasses running Android XR on Qualcomm's Snapdragon, with a 70-degree field of view, shipping this fall.

title: When deep research isn't enough for your business: Sakana AI launches 'ultra deep research' agent for 100+ page reports in 8 hours
blurb: Sakana AI launched Marlin, a "Virtual CSO" agent that runs eight-hour reasoning loops to produce 100-page strategy reports.

title: IMPACTeen: Intentions, Manipulation, Persuasion, Annotations, and Consequences in Teen Communication Dataset
blurb: IMPACTeen is a dataset of 1,021 adolescent social influence scenarios annotated from five perspectives, supporting research on manipulation and persuasion detection.

title: Anthropic's Model Shutdown Makes Continuity the New Test for AI Buyers
blurb: Anthropic disabled Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 worldwide after a Commerce Department order barred foreign nationals, making continuity a new procurement test.

title: Teleport adds LLM Proxy and Delegated Identity to secure AI agent actions and access
blurb: Teleport added LLM Proxy and Delegated Identity to its Beams beta, enforcing scoped, audited identity and access controls on AI agents.

title: Revisiting the Systematicity in Negation in the Era of In-Context Learning
blurb: This paper finds that LLMs can recognize negation cues through in-context learning but still fail at reliably identifying negation scope.

title: Databricks pitches LTAP as a new foundation for agentic applications
blurb: Databricks introduced LTAP, an architecture unifying transactional and analytical data on one lakehouse layer to give AI agents live and historical context without ETL pipelines.

title: Beyond the Blood Draw: Explainable Machine Learning for Non-Invasive Dysglycemia Risk Screening
blurb: A LightGBM model screens dysglycemia risk from NHANES data without lab tests, hitting AUC 0.820 and beating established clinical risk scores.

title: Understanding Scam Trends and Rail Paths from Reddit Self-Disclosure Narratives
blurb: A dataset of 21,304 Reddit scam narratives from 2023 to 2025 tracking yearly trends and multi-stage scam rail paths.

title: Beyond the Smile: A Hybrid Convolutional VAE for Crypto Volatility Surfaces
blurb: A convolutional VAE reconstructs crypto implied-volatility surfaces, cutting completion error eightfold over a smile re-fit at 50% masking.

title: LLM-based Visual Code Completion for Aerospace Geometric Design
blurb: This paper presents an LLM-based visual programming copilot for aerospace geometric design, built on a ReAct variant and GPT 5.4.

title: A Billion Dollars of Leveraged SpaceX Bets Hit ETFs in One Day
blurb: Leveraged bets on SpaceX generated more than $1 billion of trading volume on their first day, as investors rushed to amplify wagers on the biggest IPO in history. The appetite isn't for shares but for amplified exposure, with retail traders reaching for leveraged ETFs to multiply the move on Musk's newest public company. That kind of demand on day one signals a market less interested in valuing the business than in trading the volatility around it.

title: Riding High After I.P.O., SpaceX Will Buy A.I. Start-Up for $60 Billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to buy AI coding startup Cursor in an all-stock deal worth about $60B, days after its own blockbuster IPO. The move folds one of the fastest-growing developer tools into Elon Musk's AI push, giving him a frontier coding asset to pair with xAI. For founders, it marks how quickly post-IPO compute and equity get redeployed into AI consolidation, and how the buyers for breakout AI companies are increasingly platform giants rather than the labs that supply the models.

title: Exclusive: OpenAI Losses Increased Nearly 8X in 2025, With Spending Hitting $34 Billion
blurb: OpenAI's losses jumped nearly 8x in 2025, with the company burning $20.92B from operations on $13.07B in revenue against $34B in costs. R&D alone hit $19.18B, more than its entire top line, and the for-profit conversion added a $41.55B non-cash charge that pushed the net loss attributable to the company to $38.53B. Revenue more than tripled from $3.7B, but spending is scaling faster than sales, and the gap between what OpenAI takes in and what it spends to stay at the frontier is widening, not closing.

title: SpaceX Pounces on $60 Billion Cursor Takeover Days After IPO
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Cursor for $60B just days after Cursor's IPO, folding the AI coding company into its broadening software ambitions. The speed signals how aggressively cash-rich tech giants are now buying their way into the AI stack rather than building in-house. For founders, it also resets the comp for AI-native developer tools, putting a concrete number on what a category-leading coding company commands at exit.

title: SpaceX to acquire AI coding platform Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding platform Cursor for $60 billion in all-stock, two days after its IPO and months after absorbing xAI. The deal rescues a company that was reportedly struggling to break even as Anthropic's Claude Code took the dominant position Cursor once held. The logic is compute: Cursor said its growth was bottlenecked on infrastructure, xAI already wired it into its data centers, and the two had started co-training models like Grok Build. Owning the IDE gives SpaceX a distribution surface for those models against Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google.

title: SpaceX is public: Everything you need to know post-IPO
blurb: SpaceX completed the largest IPO in history, pricing 555.6 million shares at $135 to raise $85.7 billion and making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire. Shares opened at $150, climbed past $160, and pushed the valuation to $2.7T, fifth-most valuable company in the world and ahead of Amazon. Days later SpaceX announced a $60B all-stock deal to acquire Cursor, while COO Gwynne Shotwell floated a SpaceX-Tesla merger and banks led by Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley collected roughly $500M in fees.

title: SpaceX passes Amazon as valuation balloons to $2.7T
blurb: SpaceX briefly passed Amazon to become the world's fifth-most valuable company, with its valuation spiking to $2.9T before settling, just days after a $1.7T IPO that raised nearly $86B. The run-up has little to do with fundamentals: SpaceX lost $4.9B on $18.7B in revenue last year, while Amazon turned a $78B profit on $717B in sales. The stock is moving on the promise of a trillion-dollar AI business, fueled by the Cursor acquisition and compute-leasing deals with Anthropic and Google, even though only 4% of shares trade publicly and those deals are non-binding.

title: Plaud says its software business topped $100M in ARR after shipping over 2M AI notetakers
blurb: Plaud says its AI notetaker software business has crossed $100M in ARR after shipping more than 2M screenless devices, a rare proof point in a hardware category short on success stories. The economics come from hardware pulling subscriptions, with nearly half of device owners upgrading from the free 300-minute tier to paid pro or unlimited plans. The model is worth watching because Plaud doesn't sell standalone software, so each device sale is the top of a subscription funnel, even as Anker, Viaim, Vibe, and Pocket crowd the same market.

title: Bland, which uses proprietary, in-house-built voice models to process calls for 250+ enterprise clients, raised a $50M Series C led by Dell Technologies Capital (Lily Mae Lazarus/Fortune)
blurb: Bland raised a $50M Series C led by Dell Technologies Capital, on the back of proprietary, in-house-built voice models that now process calls for more than 250 enterprise clients. The owned-model approach is the bet: while most voice startups stitch together third-party APIs, Bland controls the stack and the margins that come with it. For founders building on voice, the signal is that enterprises are paying for systems that can be tuned, monitored, and fixed end to end, not assembled from parts a vendor can pull.

title: Trump’s Anthropic Crackdown Sets Off AI Alarms for US Allies
blurb: The Trump administration's crackdown on Anthropic has given US allies a fresh reason to worry about their standing in the AI race. When Washington can move against one of its own frontier labs on short notice, every government building national strategy on top of American models has to price in political risk it can't control. The signal to allied capitals is that access to US AI is now a lever, which is exactly the kind of dependency that accelerates sovereign-AI programs and pushes partners to hedge toward alternatives.

title: Dear SaaStr:  My Top Sales Rep Made $600,000 a Year — And Just Quit!  What Happened?
blurb: Your top sales rep usually quits because of change, not money. A new VP of Sales arrives, the old one who sheltered them leaves, or next year's number simply looks harder to hit, and they bolt even when it means starting over for less. The rep thrived in the current environment and rarely reads change as good for them. You can't fully control it, but you can align any incoming VP with your top AE and pull that rep into the hiring process before the new boss lands.

title: People prefer products with fewer ingredients
blurb: Most "organic" and "natural" claims now blur together because everyone uses them, but a simpler signal cuts through: telling buyers your product has "just a few" ingredients rather than "many" makes them up to 22% more likely to choose it. The mechanism is perceived purity, not actual ingredient count, so the win is in the framing of what you already have. If your product genuinely runs lean on ingredients, say so explicitly in the copy instead of leaning on the crowded organic and natural language.

title: Databricks Sales Double for Product Rivaling Snowflake
blurb: Databricks said its data warehousing business more than doubled over the past year to a $1.5 billion annual run rate, putting it in direct contention with Snowflake and Google for the warehouse market. The growth shows Databricks pushing beyond its lakehouse roots into the warehousing tier where Snowflake has been strongest, turning two adjacent data platforms into head-to-head rivals. For founders building on the modern data stack, the consolidation means fewer independent layers and one more category where the platform you depend on now spans your entire pipeline.

title: Snap Launches $2,195 Specs, Declaring Glasses the Next Computer
blurb: Snap launched Specs, a $2,195 pair of augmented reality glasses that co-founder Evan Spiegel is positioning as the computer that replaces the phone. The price puts it in Vision Pro territory rather than consumer reach, which signals this is a developer and early-adopter bet, not a mass-market product. The wager is that face-worn computing is the next platform shift, and whoever owns the hardware and the spatial app ecosystem early gets to set the rules the way Apple did for mobile.

title: Anthropic, Trump Officials Seek Deal on Restoring Powerful Model Access
blurb: Anthropic and Trump administration officials are negotiating to restore access to the company's most powerful models, days after Anthropic pulled Fable and Mythos. In its first public comment since the move, the company said both sides are working quickly to resolve the dispute. For founders building on frontier APIs, the episode is a reminder that model access now sits inside a political relationship, and a supplier's regulatory standing can pull capabilities out from under your product overnight.

title: Mobileye puts self-driving tech in 230 million cars. Now it wants to run its own robotaxis.
blurb: Mobileye spent 25 years as the neutral arms dealer of self-driving, with its cameras, chips, and software now in more than 230 million cars, and on 16 June it said it would start running its own robotaxis. The plan is a 100-vehicle fleet in a major US metro in 2027, scaling toward 17,000 over five years, with its Moovit app handling booking and routing. The catch is that operating its own service puts Mobileye against the customers who license Mobileye Drive, and 2027 is late against a Waymo already running hundreds of thousands of paid rides a week.

title: Y Combinator reveals a new AI agent that can build and run an entire business just from a text message
blurb: Y Combinator launched Locus Founder, an AI agent that builds and runs an internet business from a single text. Send an idea over iMessage, SMS, or Telegram, and it handles market research across 40-plus paid data APIs, brand identity, full-stack site deployment, product sourcing, and outbound marketing. It also moves money, settling sales in USDC through a non-custodial wallet with spending controls and audit trails, so the agent can earn, hold, and spend under human oversight.

title: Moody's: Private Credit Faces 2028 Test
blurb: Private credit's real test arrives in 2028, when a refinancing wave will expose which borrowers survive as software portfolios already show cracks.

title: Probably raises $9M to build a more reliable kind of AI
blurb: Probably raised $9M from a16z to catch LLM hallucinations with a deterministic validator harness that lets smaller, cheaper models hit 99.99% accuracy.

title: When deep research isn't enough for your business: Sakana AI launches 'ultra deep research' agent for 100+ page reports in 8 hours
blurb: Sakana AI launched Marlin, a B2B "Virtual CSO" agent that runs eight-hour reasoning loops to produce cited 100-page strategy reports.

title: Judge Dismisses Elon Musk’s xAI Trade-Secret Lawsuit Against OpenAI
blurb: A judge dismissed Elon Musk's xAI trade-secret suit against OpenAI, which alleged OpenAI poached an engineer who worked on its chatbot.

title: Correlated randomness in Slay the Spire 2
blurb: The author shows that Slay the Spire 2's separate pseudorandom number generators are correlated, so observing one early RNG output leaks information that predicts the others, producing oddities like a 54% chance that Neow's Bones yields the Debt curse. The root cause is initializing each generator from the same seed plus a per-purpose hash, which leaves their first outputs statistically linked rather than independent.

title: Boot Naked Linux
blurb: The author boots a minimal Linux system with nothing but a kernel and a single statically linked program running as PID 1, skipping the usual initramfs, init system, and userspace entirely. The walkthrough covers compiling the program against the raw syscall interface, pointing the kernel at it via the init= boot parameter, and testing the result under QEMU before running on real hardware.

title: Understanding the rationale behind a rule when trying to circumvent it
blurb: Microsoft's "Old New Thing" argues that following a rule's letter without grasping its intent reproduces the exact failure the rule exists to prevent. The example is a driver whose process/thread callback correctly queues slow work to a System Worker Thread, then blocks waiting for it to finish, hanging the system because the real constraint was "return fast," not "delegate the work.

title: Agentic Code Review
blurb: Code review has become the most leveraged skill in software because coding agents now produce solid code faster than any human can read it, shifting the bottleneck from writing to deciding whether to trust a change. The author argues the right review approach depends entirely on context, and points the same agents at incoming PR queues to triage diffs, turning the tools generating the extra code into the means of keeping up with it.

title: Linux 7.1 arrives with an NTFS overhaul and major hardware performance boosts
blurb: Linus Torvalds released Linux 7.1, a stable kernel headlined by a new NTFS driver, Intel FRED support for faster Panther Lake CPUs, quicker Intel Arc Battlemage graphics, and improved support for older AMD Radeon GPUs. The release also bundles USB heap-overflow fixes plus use-after-free, memory-leak, and refcount corrections across i2c, zram, gpio, and net, with the Linux 7.2 merge window now open.

title: How to Optimize Transformer-Based Models for Low-Precision Training
blurb: This NVIDIA guide shows how to translate a transformer config and batch size into the exact M×K×N GEMM shapes a model executes, then microbenchmark those shapes across BF16, FP8, and NVFP4 to predict where low precision actually pays off. Using a 5B-parameter CodonFM RNA model as the example, it leans on Transformer Engine for quantization and kernel dispatch so teams can estimate speedups on Hopper and Blackwell before committing to an expensive training run.

title: Measurement Study of Post-Quantum Readiness of Internet: 2026
blurb: A large-scale scan of 32,011 domains finds that while 49.3% now support hybrid post-quantum key exchange like ML-KEM768 with X25519, 50.7% still rely on classical key exchange and 0% deploy hybrid post-quantum certificates. The authors argue this leaves the authentication layer exposed to certificate forgery and Harvest-Now-Decrypt-Later attacks, with banking and government sectors lagging on TLS 1.2 at 15.70% of domains.

title: Why is Meta destroying its engineering organization?
blurb: Gergely Orosz argues that Meta has dismantled its two-decade high-performance engineering culture in just a few weeks, recasting software engineering from a profit center into a disdained cost center through AI-mandated workflows and pressure on core engineers. He ties the shift to leadership treating infra teams as expendable, citing a recent high-profile outage and internal disarray as early evidence the demolition is already backfiring.

title: Google launches Android 17 and Wear OS 7, first on Pixel devices, with support for the latest AI models, a bubble bar UI, and live updates on Wear OS (Sarah Perez/TechCrunch)
blurb: Google released Android 17 and Wear OS 7, debuting first on Pixel devices, with support for the latest on-device AI models, a new bubble bar UI for multitasking, and live updates that surface ongoing activity on the watch face. Developers get earlier access to the platform APIs and design changes, with broader OEM rollout expected to follow the Pixel launch.

title: Making Claude a Chemist
blurb: Anthropic chemist David Kamber tested how Claude reads NMR spectra, a chemist's most common analytical input, as part of a broader effort to make the model fluent across chemistry's representations. The post details the translation problem the work targets: chemists constantly reconcile hand-drawn structures, instrument readouts, and database notations against a registry of over 290 million substances that grows by roughly 15,000 entries a day.

title: Google Flight Simulator
blurb: Google added an experimental flight simulator to the web version of Google Earth, letting users fly over streamed 3D buildings and high-resolution imagery using keyboard controls. The mode runs in-browser only, uses simplified physics aimed at casual exploration rather than aerodynamic training, and pauses with an instant restart when the aircraft hits terrain.

title: Stanford's DeLM cuts multi-agent task costs 50% — without a central orchestrator
blurb: Stanford researchers released DeLM, a decentralized multi-agent framework that drops the central orchestrator in favor of a shared knowledge base where agents coordinate directly off one another's verified progress. The approach cuts task costs by roughly 50% by removing the merge-filter-rebroadcast bottleneck, letting agents build on prior findings and recover detailed evidence only when needed.

title: Google launches 'bubble' multi-tasking and foldable gaming mode with Android 17
blurb: Android 17 is rolling out to Pixel devices today, adding Bubbles, which turns any app into a floating window via long-press so you can view two apps over a third, plus a foldable gaming mode with a 50/50 game-and-gamepad layout and native controller remapping. The release also ships Screen Reactions for simultaneous selfie-and-screen recording, temporary per-app location grants, and a biometric remote lock in Find Hub.

title: Linux kernel 7.1 sends Intel 486 support to silicon heaven
blurb: The Linux kernel 7.1 release drops support for Intel's 80486 and early 586 processors, removing decades-old workarounds for CPUs that lack the CMPXCHG8B and TSC instructions modern x86 code assumes. The cleanup deletes legacy emulation paths from the kernel's boot and locking code, simplifying maintenance for the 32-bit x86 architecture that remains in embedded and retro-computing use.

title: Wear OS 7 helps your smartwatch keep up with you
blurb: Wear OS 7 is rolling out to eligible Pixel Watch devices, adding Android Live Updates on the wrist, cross-device media and earbud controls, and system-level power optimizations that deliver up to 10% better battery life over Wear OS 6. Select devices get Gemini Intelligence later this year, including natural-language widget creation via Create My Widget and multi-step app automation that runs tasks directly from the watch.

title: Accelerate search queries with full-text search indexes on Databricks
blurb: Databricks added full-text search indexes in Beta on Databricks Runtime 18.2, accelerating substring and keyword queries on open-format tables by 100x or more without changing table layouts. Created with a single SQL statement and stored separately from the base table, the index maps tokens to matching rows so the query engine skips most files automatically, replacing workarounds like duplicate tables or external systems such as Elasticsearch and Splunk.

title: SpaceX purchases Cursor, a Claude Code and OpenAI Codex competitor [U]
blurb: SpaceX acquired Cursor in a $60 billion deal, a week after the company went public and roughly two months after an initial $10 billion partnership granting Cursor access to xAI's Colossus infrastructure. The acquisition pulls a leading agentic coding tool, and a direct competitor to Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex, into SpaceX's expanding AI portfolio alongside xAI.

title: Firefox 152 understands “Sssh!”
blurb: Firefox 152 adds a Quick Action that silences every audio-playing tab across all windows when users type "mute," "sssh," or "hush" into the address bar. The release also ships experimental JPEG XL support, multi-tab sending and URL copying to other devices, a redesigned Settings layout, and roughly 40 security fixes.

title: Android 17 arrives on Pixel phones today
blurb: Google began rolling out Android 17 to compatible Pixel phones today, with other manufacturers expected to ship the update through 2026. The release adds Bubbles for floating app windows with a dedicated bubble bar on foldables and tablets, native controller remapping across all Android phones, foldable gaming controls, and Pixel-exclusive additions including AirDrop support via Quick Share on the 8A and 9A.

title: I admire Fabrice Bellard. He is almost certainly a better overall programmer
blurb: John Carmack praised Fabrice Bellard, the prolific French engineer behind FFmpeg, QEMU, and QuickJS, as almost certainly a better overall programmer than himself.

title: Google Chrome update will close the door on ad blockers
blurb: Google is removing the final Manifest V2 workaround in Chrome 150, expected June 30, fully ending support for MV2 extensions including ad blockers like uBlock Origin. The change deletes the `ExtensionManifestV2Disabled` flag as dead code, with remaining MV2 flags slated for Chrome 151 in July, and Chromium-based browsers like Edge and Opera expected to follow.

title: ICYMI: OpenAI released CDP support for browser use on Codex
blurb: OpenAI added Chrome DevTools Protocol access to Codex, letting the agent profile JavaScript performance, read console output, network traffic, and rendered state, and rewrite a page's DOM directly from its embedded browser or Chrome. The opt-in developer mode is gated behind a Settings toggle, excluded from the EEA, UK, and Switzerland at launch, and still runs slowly and undertrained on the tooling, often needing several prompting attempts.

title: Apple reveals why macOS might block your Terminal prompt
blurb: macOS 26.4 blocks Terminal pastes for infrequent users when commands are copied from websites, chats, or email to curb malware.

title: Researchers recycle old phones and cluster them into ‘computing platforms’ that operate as a low-cost data center — says processors on modern smartphones deliver higher single-core performance than comparable multicore servers
blurb: UCSD and Google stripped retired Pixel phones to bare motherboards, ran Linux and Kubernetes, and clustered 25–50 into a server-class node.

title: Android 17 starts hitting Pixel phones and watches today
blurb: Android 17 begins rolling out to Pixel phones and watches, adding a Bubbles system that opens any app as a floating window.

title: The Proxy Knows Too Much: Sealing LLM API Routers with Attested TEEs
blurb: AEGIS runs LLM API routers inside attested hardware enclaves, blocking malicious proxies from rewriting tool calls or exfiltrating plaintext, at roughly 6ms overhead.

title: Cancelled Xbox 360 version of GoldenEye 007 gets recompiled for PC — ‘No emulator, the game runs as a real native executable,’ insists dev
blurb: GoldEye Recomp v1.0 statically recompiles the cancelled Xbox 360 GoldenEye 007 into a native C++ Windows executable, no emulator required.

title: What’s new with Unity Catalog at Data + AI Summit 2026
blurb: Databricks expanded Unity Catalog at Data + AI Summit 2026 with the Unity AI Gateway, which extends governance from data and AI assets to runtime interactions across models, agents, MCP servers, and tools. Other additions include a Glossary and Domains for shared business context, cross-cloud and cross-region addressability, and a Governance Hub command center, with more than 14,000 organizations now governing data and AI on the platform.

title: Databricks unveils CustomerLake, its agentic CDP
